content:
Yesterday we looked at the silver plate 130 shekels, flat and open, the publicly displayed redemption price of Christ, bearing the message that out of fallen man at 130 years, a new beginning was appointed. Today we move to the second silver vessel that each tribal leader brought: the silver bowl. It sat beside the plate on the altar, smaller in width but deeper in form, and it carries its own profound message about the nature of Christ’s sacrifice and about the reach of His redemption to every corner of the earth.

The plate was flat and wide, its contents visible and displayed. The bowl was different. A bowl is deeper than a plate designed to hold, preserve, and contain what is placed in it without anything spilling or being lost. The grain offering of fine flour and oil that filled the bowl was the same as what filled the plate, but the bowl held it differently. Nothing could fall off the edges. Nothing could be scattered by the wind. What the bowl received, it kept.

This difference between the plate and the bowl speaks to the difference between two aspects of Christ’s sacrifice. The plate speaks of the public, open, displayed nature of the ransom; the cross laid before all eyes. The bowl speaks of the depth from which that sacrifice came. Christ’s offering was not a flat obedience a mechanical compliance with what the Law required, carried out because there was no other option. It came from the depth of His heart, from the settled will of His inner life, from a love and a surrender that went far below the surface.

Hebrews 10:7 gives us Christ’s own voice as He entered the world: “Behold, I have come to do Your will, O God, as it is written of me in the scroll of the book.” This was not a reluctant statement dragged out of Him under compulsion. It was the declaration of a heart that delighted in the will of the Father. Psalm 40:8, the same passage quoted in Hebrews, says: “I delight to do Your will, O my God; Your law is within my heart.” The bowl is deep because the delight was real. The sacrifice that redeemed us didn’t not come as compulsion, it was an offering made from the depths of a willing, loving, fully surrendered heart.

The depth of the bowl also speaks of the fact that Christ faced everything that lay ahead of Him and did not turn away. In the Garden of Gethsemane,
He prayed in the most honest anguish: “not as I will, but as You will” (Matthew 26:39). The weight of what He was about to bear was real. The trembling was real. And yet He rose from prayer and walked forward. Luke 9:51 says: “When the days drew near for Him to be taken up, He set His face to go to Jerusalem.” Set His face. Not dragged. Not driven. He turned toward what He knew was waiting and walked into it.

Isaiah 50:5-7 gives us the voice of the Servant before the cross: “The Lord GOD has opened my ear, and I was not rebellious; I turned not backward. I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting. But the Lord GOD helps me; therefore I have not been disgraced; therefore I have set my face like a flint.” A face set like a flint. Not turned back. Not hidden. The bowl is deep because the surrender went all the way down; past the fear, past the pain, past the disgrace, to the very bottom of what the Father required.

John 10:17-18 removes any question about whether the sacrifice was voluntary: “For this reason the Father loves me, because I lay down my life that I may take it up again. No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it up again.” No one took it from Him. He laid it down. The bowl holds what is placed in it willingly.
The depth of the silver bowl is the depth of a voluntary love that could have withheld itself and chose not to.

The silver bowl weighed 70 shekels. In Scripture, seventy is consistently the number of the nations. Genesis 10 lists the seventy nations of the world that descended from Noah’s sons after the flood; seventy families, seventy languages, seventy branches of the human family spread across the earth. When Moses appointed elders to help carry the burden of the people in the wilderness, he appointed seventy (Numbers 11:16). When Christ sent out the disciples ahead of Him, He sent seventy (Luke 10:1). Seventy speaks of the full scope of humanity; not one nation but all nations, not one tribe but the whole world.

The silver bowl at 70 shekels is saying that the redemption Christ paid; this deep, voluntary, whole-hearted sacrifice - was not for Israel only. It was for all nations. And because it was a bowl, not one of those nations would be dropped or scattered or lost. What the bowl holds, it keeps. John 3:16 says: “For God so loved the world.” Not one tribe. The world. Revelation 5:9 gives us the worship of the redeemed around the throne: “You were slain, and by Your blood You ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation.” The 70 shekels of the silver bowl are all of them, held safely, not one spilled.

The silver plate and the silver bowl stood together before the altar; the public display and the depth of heart, the open cost and the gathered nations. They were brought by each of the twelve leaders, one by one, over twelve days. But here is a question that we have not yet answered. When we add the plate and the bowl together, 130 and 70, we get 200 shekels per leader. And twelve leaders give us a total of 2,400 shekels of silver. What does 200 mean? What does 2,400 mean? And why does it matter? That is what we will study tomorrow.

title: Prayer
text:
Yahweh, we thank You that the sacrifice of Christ was not a flat obedience but a deep surrender; that He set His face like a flint, that He laid down His life of His own accord, that He delighted to do Your will even when it was bitter.

We thank You that the silver bowl at 70 shekels holds every nation, every tongue, every people that the depth of His sacrifice was wide enough for all and deep enough that not one would be lost. Forgive us for the shallowness of our own surrender; the obedience that goes only as far as it is comfortable and no further. Deepen the bowl in us. Help us to offer not just the visible plate of our outward lives but the deeper surrender of the heart from which true sacrifice comes. In Christ’s name, amen.
